info.topictitle: D.2.2 Magnetic field properties and laws

markdown:
## Magnetic Field Lines


### Patterns Around Magnets


Magnetic field lines **visually represent **the direction and strength of a magnetic field.

<callout type="example">Magnetic field lines **always **point from the **north **pole to the **south **pole **outside **a magnet, and from **south **to **north inside **the magnet, forming **closed loops**.</callout>

![Magnetic field lines around a bar magnet, directed from the north pole to the south pole.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/863b1129ec35c913250bc54abb8cb422a678a30a-2000x1000.png)

<callout type="example">* Consider a bar magnet.
* If you sprinkle iron filings around it, the filings align along the magnetic field lines, revealing a pattern that is **densest **near the poles (where the field is strongest) and** spreads out **as you move away.</callout>

<embed type="" url="https://phet.colorado.edu/sims/html/magnet-and-compass/latest/magnet-and-compass_all.html">Magnetic Field around a Bar Magnet </embed>

### Patterns Around Wires


1. A **straight current-carrying wire **generates a magnetic field with lines forming **concentric circles **around the wire.
2. The **right-hand rule **helps determine the direction:
  1. Point your **thumb **in the direction of the **current**.
  2. Your **fingers **will curl in the direction of the **magnetic field lines**.

<callout type="example">If the current flows upward, the magnetic field lines will circle the wire in a **counterclockwise **direction.</callout>

![Right hand grip rule showing concentric magnetic field lines around a straight current carrying wire.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/47dd2d49130d44e8b9457c9e818e54d877f7b913-1146x677.png)

### Patterns Around Solenoids


1. A **solenoid **is a coil of wire that produces a magnetic field similar to a bar magnet when current flows through it.
2. Inside the solenoid, the field lines are **parallel **and **uniform**, indicating a **strong **and **constant **magnetic field.
3. Outside, the lines resemble those of a bar magnet, exiting from one end (the **north **pole) and entering the other (the **south **pole).

<callout type="tip">To find the direction of the magnetic field in a solenoid, use the **right-hand grip rule**, curl your **fingers **in the direction of the **current**, and your **thumb **will point toward the **solenoid’s north pole**.</callout>

![Magnetic field lines around a current carrying solenoid, with current direction shown.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/c15c9b5845dc23a6b651f75c7af024e65eb8ffaf-1042x912.png)

## Force on a Moving Charge


1. When a charged particle moves through a magnetic field, it experiences a force called the **magnetic force**.
2. The magnitude of this force is given by the formula: $$F = qvB \sin \theta$$ where:
  1. $F$ is the magnetic force.
  2. $q$ is the charge of the particle.
  3. $v$ is the velocity of the particle.
  4. $B$ is the magnetic flux density (strength of the magnetic field).
  5. $\theta$ is the angle between the velocity vector and the magnetic field vector.

<callout type="example">If a proton ($q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \, \text{C}$) moves at $2 \times 10^6 \, \text{m s}^{-1}$ through a magnetic field of $0.5 \, \text{T}$ at an angle of $90^\circ$, the **force **is:

$$F = (1.6 \times 10^{-19} \, \text{C})(2 \times 10^6 \, \text{m s}^{-1})(0.5 \, \text{T}) \sin 90^\circ $$

$$= 1.6 \times 10^{-13} \, \text{N}$$</callout>

<callout type="warning">Students often forget that the magnetic force is **zero **if the velocity is **parallel **to the magnetic field ($\theta = 0^\circ$ or $180^\circ$).</callout>

<callout type="tip">Use the **right-hand rule **to determine the direction of the force on a positive charge:

* Point your **fingers **in the direction of the velocity ($v$).
* Align your **palm **with the magnetic field ($B$).
* Your thumb will point in the direction of the force ($F$). For a negative charge, the force is in the opposite direction.For a negative charge, the force is in the **opposite **direction.</callout>

## Force on a Current-Carrying Wire


1. A wire carrying an electric **current **in a **magnetic field **also experiences a **force**.
2. The magnitude of this force is given by: $$F = BIL \sin \theta$$ where:
  1. $F$ is the magnetic force.
  2. $B$ is the magnetic flux density.
  3. $I$ is the current in the wire.
  4. $L$ is the length of the wire in the magnetic field.
  5. $\theta$ is the angle between the current direction and the magnetic field.

<callout type="example">Consider a wire carrying a current of $3 \, \text{A}$ in a magnetic field of $0.4 \, \text{T}$. If the wire is $0.5 \, \text{m}$ long and the angle between the current and the field is $60^\circ$, the **force **is:

$$F = (0.4 \, \text{T})(3 \, \text{A})(0.5 \, \text{m}) \sin 60^\circ $$

$$\approx 0.52 \, \text{N}$$</callout>

<callout type="tip">To find the direction of the force, use the **right-hand rule **for currents:

* Point your **thumb **in the direction of the current ($I$).
* Align your **palm **with the magnetic field ($B$).
* Your **fingers **will point in the direction of the force ($F$).</callout>

## Force Between Parallel Wires


1. **Two parallel wires** carrying currents exert forces **on each other** due to their magnetic fields.
2. The **force per unit length** between two wires is given by: $$\frac{F}{L} = \frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r}$$ where:
  1. $F$ is the force between the wires.
  2. $L$ is the length of the wires.
  3. $\mu_0$ is the permeability of free space ($4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \text{N A}^{-2}$).
  4. $I_1$ and $I_2$ are the currents in the wires.
  5. $r$ is the distance between the wires.

![Magnetic field patterns around two parallel current carrying wires showing that currents in the same direction attract and opposite directions repel.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/174c815481b52005500e6d96ee1bcf9428658b08-1511x729.png)

![Forces between two parallel current carrying wires, attraction for currents in the same direction and repulsion for currents in opposite directions.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/83556982bfd4c98fc7e6f04dae1d86383cd9f6ad-2550x1611.png)

<callout type="example">If two wires, each carrying a current of $5 \, \text{A}$, are separated by $0.1 \, \text{m}$, the force per unit length is:

$$\frac{F}{L} = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \text{T m A}^{-1})(5 \, \text{A})(5 \, \text{A})}{2 \pi (0.1 \, \text{m})}$$

$$ = 5 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{N m}^{-1}$$</callout>

<callout type="hint">The force between the wires is **attractive **if the currents flow in the **same **direction and **repulsive **if they flow in **opposite **directions.</callout>

<callout type="self_review">* How do magnetic field lines differ around a bar magnet, a straight wire, and a solenoid?
* What happens to the magnetic force on a charge if the angle $\theta$ between its velocity and the magnetic field is $0^\circ$?
* How does the direction of current affect the force between two parallel wires?</callout>
